title: Parametrically Driven iSWAP Gate Using a Capacitively Shunted Double-Transmon Coupler at the Zero-Flux Sweet Spot
authors: Shinichi Inoue, Rui Li, Kentaro Kubo, Yinghao Ho, Yasunobu Nakamura, Hayato Goto
abstract: A double-transmon coupler (DTC) enables a fast, high-fidelity CZ gate between two highly detuned, fixed-frequency transmon qubits. Moreover, a recently proposed capacitively shunted DTC (CSDTC) realizes a small residual ZZ interaction over a wide flux-bias range around zero flux, eliminating the necessity of static flux biasing while maintaining high CZ-gate fidelity. However, CZ gates with the DTC and CSDTC require baseband flux pulses with large amplitudes, which are vulnerable to pulse distortion and decoherence due to large qubit-coupler hybridization. To address these issues, we experimentally demonstrate a parametrically driven iSWAP gate operated at zero flux bias between highly detuned, fixed-frequency transmon qubits coupled through a CSDTC. Using a simple flux-drive waveform without predistortion, we realize an average gate fidelity of 99.92(2)% at a total gate time of 112 ns. The observed high-fidelity performance is consistent with small qubit-coupler hybridization and small effective ZZ interaction during the gate. Our numerical simulations reproduce the experimentally observed iSWAP interaction rate and effective ZZ interaction, demonstrating the applicability of the theoretical model not only to spectral information but also to time-domain dynamics such as gate operations. These results boost further progress in the research of superconducting quantum computers.
summary: This paper demonstrates a high-fidelity iSWAP gate between fixed-frequency transmons using a parametric flux drive on a specialized coupler. By operating at the zero-flux sweet spot, the researchers significantly reduced decoherence and parasitic ZZ interactions. This approach provides a scalable path toward high-fidelity entangling gates in superconducting quantum processors.
main_problem: High-fidelity two-qubit gates in superconducting circuits are often limited by pulse distortion, decoherence from qubit-coupler hybridization, and residual ZZ interactions caused by large flux-pulse amplitudes.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a high-fidelity (99.92%) parametrically driven iSWAP gate at the zero-flux sweet spot, achieving reduced error by minimizing qubit-coupler hybridization and canceling ZZ interactions.
method: The researchers used a capacitively shunted double-transmon coupler (CSDTC) with a parametric flux drive, employing randomized benchmarking and quantum process tomography for characterization and virtual-Z gates for phase error compensation.
model_or_system: The system consists of two highly detuned, fixed-frequency transmon qubits coupled via a Capacitively Shunted Double-Transmon Coupler (CSDTC) that hybridizes into symmetric and antisymmetric modes.
key_observables: Average gate fidelity, iSWAP interaction rate, effective ZZ interaction strength, leakage error, and qubit-coupler hybridization fraction.
important_parameters: Gate fidelity of 99.92(2)%, gate time of 112 ns, qubit detuning of 498 MHz, and a hybridization fraction of approximately 0.05.
main_assumptions: The calibration of the $|11angle$ state was omitted because it does not affect populations in the single-excitation subspace; the model assumes the validity of the three-level approximation for dynamical ZZ interaction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device micrograph, circuit diagram, and energy spectrum; Figure 2 displays chevron patterns and interaction rates; Figure 3 presents error components; Figure 6 shows the pulse sequence; Figure 14 shows Pauli Transfer Matrices; Figure 15 shows IQ readout clusters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the hardware architecture and the problem of flux-induced errors, describes the parametric driving mechanism, presents experimental verification of the iSWAP gate and its fidelity, analyzes the error budget and ZZ suppression, and concludes with a detailed calibration and characterization framework.
why_interesting: This work is relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics due to its focus on controlling light-matter-like interactions (qubit-coupler hybridization) and managing decoherence through parametric modulation and frequency-domain engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Device and operating principle of the parametrically driven iSWAP gate at the zero-flux sweet spot. (a) False-colored optical micrograph. In the present work, we use the same device as in Ref. 28. (b) Circuit diagram of the device. Black crosses represent Josephson junctions. The device has two fixed-frequency transmon qubits (Q1 and Q2) coupled via a CSDTC. The CSDTC consists of two transmons (C3 and C4), which hybridize into two internal modes (the P mode and the M mode) through the interaction mediated by a Josephson junction (JJ5) and a shunt capacitor. A local magnetic flux Φex generated by a dedicated bias line (green) threads the coupler loop. Microwave flux drive is applied...

FIG. 2. Characterization of parametrically driven iSWAP interaction. (a) Chevron patterns of the excited-state popu- lations of Q1 and Q2, plotted as a function of the frequency ωd and pulse duration of the flux drive with the amplitude φd/2π = 0.28. The initial state is |01⟩. (b) Experimentally measured iSWAP interaction strength giSWAP and (c) reso- nant drive frequency ωres d as functions of the squared flux- drive amplitude φ2 d, together with numerical simulation re- sults (black solid curves) and analytic prediction, giSWAP (or- ange dashed line) given by Eq. (23).

FIG. 3. Randomized benchmarking of the iSWAP gate with a total gate duration ttot of 112 ns. (a) Sequence fidelity P| g 0000⟩−Pχ1/4 as a function of the number of Clifford gates. The dashed lines are fits to Eq. (28). (b) Leakage randomized benchmarking. The dashed lines are fits to Eq. (30).

FIG. 4. (a) Experimentally measured iSWAP depolarization- induced error rD iSWAP, coherent ZZ error rZZ, and leakage error L1 as a function of the total iSWAP gate duration ttot. The blue dashed line denotes gate infidelities estimated from the qubit coherence times measured at zero flux bias. (b) Effective coherence time T D (blue dots) inferred from the depolarization-induced error for each total gate duration ttot. The blue dashed line shows the effective coherence time es- timated from the same qubit coherence properties as in (a), and the shaded blue band indicates the ±1σ uncertainty. The red and purple markers show the effective coherence times re- ported for CZ gates using a DTC...

FIG. 5. (a) Optimized flux-drive waveform for the iSWAP gate (ttot = 112 ns), together with the symmetric part of the BNZ waveform used to implement a CZ gate (ttot = 80 ns) on the same device in Ref. 28. (b) Numerically simulated coupler hybridization fraction as a function of time. The solid blue and red lines represent the hybridization fractions for the iSWAP- and CZ-gate waveforms in (a), respectively.
